EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 4 - Homework: External GPIO Control

Bare-Metal GPIO with External Components

Author

Basri Erdogan

1 Homework Overview

Lecture 4’s lab used direct CMSIS register access to control the on-board LED on PA5. For this homework you will transfer the same clock, mode, input, and output reasoning to external components wired to the Nucleo-F401RE headers.

2 Learning Outcomes

  • Configure multiple GPIO ports using direct CMSIS register writes.
  • Implement reliable digital input handling with a discrete push button.
  • Demonstrate good embedded development practice through documentation and verification artifacts.

3 Required Materials

  • ST Nucleo-F401RE development board
  • Breadboard and jumper wires
  • 1 × 5 mm LED (any colour)
  • 1 × resistor between 220 Ω and 330 Ω (current limiting for the LED)
  • 1 × Momentary push button (four-leg tactile switch or similar)
  • USB cable to power/program the board

4 External Wiring Guide

Follow the steps below before powering the board. The Arduino-compatible headers on the Nucleo make it easy to access the required pins.

  1. LED circuit (Port B):
    • Connect the LED anode to header pin A3, which maps to MCU pin PB0.
    • Place the 220–330 Ω resistor between the LED cathode and the ground rail.
    • Run a jumper from the ground rail back to any GND pin on the Nucleo (e.g. next to A2).
    • Result: driving PB0 high will forward-bias the LED through the resistor.
  2. Push button circuit (Port C):
    • Choose A4 on the header (MCU pin PC1) as your digital input.
    • Tie one leg of the button to A4 (PC1).
    • Tie the opposite leg of the button directly to any GND pin.
    • You will enable the internal pull-up resistor in software so the input idles high; pressing the button connects PC1 to ground and reads as low.
  3. Power rails:
    • Use the 3.3 V and GND headers on the Nucleo to power the breadboard rails.
    • Double-check there are no direct shorts between 3.3 V and GND before connecting the board to USB.

5 Development Tasks

5.1 Task 1 — Project Setup & LED Check

  1. Copy your completed L04 laboratory project and rename the copy for the homework.
  2. Confirm that the project targets the STM32F401RE and that main.h makes the CMSIS names RCC, GPIOB, and GPIOC available.
  3. In main.c:
    • Enable the clock for both GPIOB and GPIOC with RCC->AHB1ENR bits 1 and 2.
    • Configure PB0 as a general-purpose output by writing 01 to MODER bits 1:0. You may leave the output type, speed, and pull-up/down at their default reset values.
    • Configure PC1 as a digital input (MODER bits 3:2 = 00) and set its internal pull-up (PUPDR bits 3:2 = 01) to match the grounding button wiring.

Use expressions such as GPIOB->MODER, GPIOB->BSRR, GPIOC->MODER, GPIOC->PUPDR, and GPIOC->IDR. Document the register fields and bit masks you use as code comments. Keep comments focused on intent (for example, // PB0 -> LED output).
Add a short delay loop that toggles PB0 on and off three times after reset to confirm your wiring.

5.2 Task 2 — Button-Controlled LED Logic

Inside the while(1) loop:

  • Sample the PC1 input each iteration (1 = button released, 0 = button pressed because of the pull-up).
  • Turn the LED on while the button is pressed and off when it is released.

6 Testing & Documentation Expectations

  • Verify the LED turns on during a button press and remains off when released.
  • If you make any wiring adjustments (different pins, inverted logic), add a short comment block describing the change.

7 Submission Checklist

  • Export the exact contents of your main.c into a plain-text file named EE5203_Lecture4_<StudentNumber>_main.c (example: EE5203_Lecture4_2104567_main.c).

  • At the top of the file include:

    // Student Number: <StudentNumber>
// Video Evidence: <URL to active video link>

    Replace the placeholders with your student number and a working URL (Google Drive, YouTube, etc.) showing the hardware functioning.

  • Submit the text file and ensure the video link remains accessible until grading is complete. No additional archives are required.

8 Safety & Troubleshooting

  • Disconnect USB power before modifying the breadboard wiring.
  • Use the board’s 3.3 V rail only—do not power the external LED from 5 V.
  • If the LED never lights, double-check that PB0 is toggling (probe with a multimeter) and confirm the resistor orientation.
  • If the button input floats or chatters excessively, revisit your pull-up/down strategy and debounce logic.

Good luck! Bring your questions to the next lab session, and be ready to show both your wiring and firmware decisions.
